healthy_option: Ezekiel bread (frozen organic sprouted, no preservatives — just sprouted grains, lentils, soybeans)
processed_option: Commercial sliced bread (preservatives like calcium propionate, DATEM, soybean oil, added sugar)
key_fact: Sprouted grain bread increases nutrient bioavailability by 30-50% and breaks down phytic acid, which blocks mineral absorption in regular bread
visual_idea: Compare ingredient lists side by side — 6 ingredients vs. 30+. Show that Ezekiel bread lives in the freezer because it has zero preservatives

healthy_option: Cream-top (non-homogenized) whole milk and yogurt from grass-fed cows
processed_option: Ultra-pasteurized homogenized milk and yogurt (fat globules broken up, shelf-stable)
key_fact: Homogenization breaks fat into tiny particles that may bypass digestion differently; cream-top milk preserves natural fat structure. Non-homogenized milk from grass-fed cows has higher omega-3 and CLA content
visual_idea: Shake a cream-top bottle to show the cream layer — 'this is what real milk looks like before the factory processes it'

processed_option: Conventional grain-fed feedlot beef (corn, soy, antibiotics, growth hormones)
key_fact: Grass-fed beef has 2-5x more omega-3 fatty acids, higher CLA (conjugated linoleic acid), and more antioxidant vitamins A and E than grain-fed. It also has a healthier omega-6 to omega-3 ratio
visual_idea: Show the color difference — deep red grass-fed vs. pale grain-fed. Compare the fat: yellow (beta-carotene from grass) vs. white

healthy_option: Organic pasture-raised eggs (hens roaming on grass, eating insects and plants)
processed_option: Conventional cage-free or battery-cage eggs (confined hens, soy/corn feed, no outdoor access)
key_fact: Pasture-raised eggs have 2x more omega-3s, 3x more vitamin D, and 6x more vitamin E. The deep orange yolk indicates higher beta-carotene from the hens' natural diet
visual_idea: Crack both eggs into a pan — compare the pale yellow conventional yolk vs. the deep orange pasture-raised yolk

healthy_option: Organic fruits (especially the Dirty Dozen: strawberries, apples, grapes, peaches, etc.)
processed_option: Conventionally grown fruits sprayed with synthetic pesticides, fungicides, and wax coatings
key_fact: The EWG Dirty Dozen shows that conventional strawberries can contain residues of 20+ different pesticides. Organic farming prohibits synthetic pesticides and has been linked to higher antioxidant levels
visual_idea: Wash a conventional apple — show how water beads off the wax coating. 'That shiny coating isn't natural'

healthy_option: Wild-caught salmon (sockeye, king, coho — naturally fed on krill and smaller fish)
processed_option: Farm-raised salmon (crowded pens, antibiotics, artificial color added to feed — astaxanthin from petrochemicals)
key_fact: Farm-raised salmon is dyed pink via synthetic astaxanthin in their feed — without it the flesh is gray. Wild salmon gets its color naturally from eating krill. Farm-raised also has higher PCB and dioxin levels
visual_idea: Show a 'color chart' that salmon farms use to pick the exact shade of pink for their fish — 'they literally choose the color like paint swatches'

healthy_option: Organic salad greens (lettuce, spinach, kale, arugula — grown without synthetic chemicals)
processed_option: Conventional salad greens (sprayed with pesticides, often pre-washed with chlorine solutions)
key_fact: Leafy greens are on the Dirty Dozen list because their large surface area absorbs more pesticide residue. Spinach samples had an average of 7 different pesticide residues. Organic greens also tend to have higher polyphenol content
visual_idea: Show the triple-wash label on a conventional bag — 'washed 3 times and there's still pesticide residue. Imagine what's on the unwashed ones'

healthy_option: Filtered water (reverse osmosis, carbon block, or Berkey-type gravity filter — removes chlorine, fluoride, lead, PFAS)
processed_option: Unfiltered tap water (may contain chlorine, fluoride, lead from pipes, microplastics, pharmaceutical residues, PFAS forever chemicals)
key_fact: The EWG Tap Water Database shows that most US tap water contains detectable levels of PFAS, microplastics, and disinfection byproducts. A quality filter can remove 95-99% of contaminants
visual_idea: Test tap water vs. filtered water with a TDS meter — show the number difference on screen. 'These are the dissolved solids you're drinking every day'

healthy_option: Glass food storage containers and stainless steel water bottles
processed_option: Plastic food containers and water bottles (BPA, BPS, phthalates — especially when heated or scratched)
key_fact: Heating plastic containers (even BPA-free) releases endocrine-disrupting microplastics into food. A study found that microwaving plastic releases billions of nanoplastic particles per serving. Glass and steel are chemically inert
visual_idea: Put a plastic container next to a glass one — 'one leaches chemicals when heated, one doesn't. Which one is cheaper long-term?'

healthy_option: Stainless steel, cast iron, and ceramic-coated cookware
processed_option: Teflon (PTFE) non-stick pans and coated appliances (release toxic fumes above 500°F, coating degrades into PFAS)
key_fact: Non-stick coatings contain PFAS (forever chemicals) that accumulate in the body. Overheated Teflon releases toxic fumes that can kill pet birds and cause polymer fume fever in humans. Cast iron actually adds beneficial iron to your food
visual_idea: Show scratched non-stick pan — 'see those scratches? That coating is now in your food and your body. It never breaks down — that's why they're called forever chemicals'
